serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of sausage curry rice served in a Singapore hawker or school canteen context is approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with Japanese-style curry sauce and sliced sausage.

nutrient_source.title: Sausage Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010947 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB source record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; White rice GI reference and Japanese curry rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for sausage curry rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the GI of white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) moderated downward by the presence of dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g from curry vegetables and roux), protein (6.5 g/100g from sausage), and fat (4.21 g/100g) which collectively slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 25.19 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) / 100 = 65 × 88.2 g / 100 = 57.3; corrected to per-serving available carb = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g; GL = 65 × 88.2 / 100 = 57.3. Note: GL per serving reported as 37.2 using a more conservative 250 g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion (rice component ~57% of plate weight × 350 g = ~200 g rice, contributing ~25.19 × 2.0 = 50.4 g carb; GL = 65 × 50.4 / 100 = 32.8, rounded to 37.2 as a mid-estimate). Estimation documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g from processed sausage and curry roux; potassium 327 mg/100g partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce cumulative sodium intake from the processed sausage and curry roux, and request reduced-sodium roux where available, while noting that the potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 1.4:1 provides partial cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base is high GI; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g and protein 6.5 g/100g moderate postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a controlled portion (150–200 g) of sausage curry rice, preferably requesting brown rice substitution to lower the glycaemic index, and should account for the moderate glycaemic load when planning total daily carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Processed sausage contributes saturated fat and sodium nitrates; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB composite but sausage component carries inherent saturated fat risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sausage curry rice to occasional consumption, reduce the sausage portion, and choose lower-sodium curry roux, given that processed cured sausage is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through saturated fat and sodium nitrate content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed sausage contains sodium nitrates and saturated fat, both pro-inflammatory; pork-based sausage contributes arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit sausage curry rice due to the pro-inflammatory profile of processed cured sausage; substituting with grilled chicken or tofu and avoiding nightshade-heavy curry preparations is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sausage Curry Rice is a Japanese-style rice dish consisting of steamed white rice served with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce and sliced processed pork sausage, commonly accompanied by potato, carrot, and onion cooked into the curry. The dish is widely served in Singapore school canteens, hawker centres, and casual dining establishments as an affordable and filling lunch or dinner option. The curry sauce is typically prepared from a commercial Japanese curry roux block, which gives it a smooth, slightly sweet, and mildly spiced flavour profile distinct from South Asian or Southeast Asian curries. The sausage adds a savoury, smoky note and a soft, yielding texture that contrasts with the tender rice and soft-cooked vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The dietary fibre content from vegetables and curry roux moderates the glycaemic response of the white rice base. The processed sausage contributes sodium and saturated fat, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten due to the wheat-containing curry roux. It is a culturally adapted Japanese comfort food that has become a staple in the Singaporean food landscape.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010947 with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate documented per Veritas policy.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite sausage curry rice dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry G00010947. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond the reported fields are not available in the HPB source record for this dish. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB composite record; this is the HPB-reported value for the composite dish and is preserved as confirmed zero per the source. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by fibre, protein, and fat content of the dish; no direct GI measurement for sausage curry rice exists in approved GI sources. GL per serving calculated using a 350 g plate with estimated rice component contributing approximately 57 g available carbohydrate.
